title: RA.1011 Carrier

translation.content: Hear the word "Dionysus" and you probably picture the Greek god of ecstasy: overflowing tables, delirious revelry, chaos. Not the austere soundworld of Guy Brewer, AKA Carrier. On the surface, the UK artist's latest alias feels almost Spartan. But look closer and the Dionysian link starts to show: the project is about shedding fixed forms and identities, to allow something more true, more vivid, to form. RA.1011 marks Brewer's third entry in the RA Mix series, following editions as drum & bass duo Commix (RA.269) and, later, under the techno alias Shifted (RA.310). "I guess it's an effort to step away from purism," Brewer told Resident Advisor back in 2023. "Right now the thresholds between genres are where you find the most exciting music." Carrier's phenomenal debut album, Rhythm Immortal, delivers on that promise. Low-end pressure cloaks like foreboding shadows, punctured by eerie, otherworldly percussion comparable only to Photek or T++. Listen to the LP in full and it feels like walking through a scene in a true-crime drama: fog-drenched city streets in the deep of night, ambushed by gusts of wind, whispers and strange noises—and it all sounds totally original. Tracing a line through dub pressure, fractured percussion and narcotic ambience, Brewer explores that same world on RA.1011. As with the album, there's a primal pulse that threads through the recording. Walls of negative space seem to hover before dissolving inexplicably, their tension intact; drums move more like the weather than rhythm. In letting go of form and his own past identities, Brewer finds something more fluid, more arresting and more alive. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? It's been quite busy with the album release, travelling for shows, and keeping up with life's more mundane tasks and responsibilities. Right now I'm sitting in Mexico City having a coffee in La Condesa. I've got a gig here and decided to turn the trip into a little holiday too. After this, I'll head to New York, then back to Europe for shows in Amsterdam and Manchester. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The mix was recorded at home in Antwerp, in my studio, on a borrowed pair of XDJ1000s and an ancient Allen & Heath mixer that's well overdue for retirement. It's now been used for three Resident Advisor mixes, a fabric live CD and a lot of other things. I wanted to do something that represents where my head is musically right now, or at least a small part of it. I wanted the mix to feel left of centre, fidgety and loose, but also have a sense of propulsion and sexiness to it. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Going back into the studio with a clean slate, no pressure or clear idea of what I want to do, and just experimenting until a path forward appears. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? The genocide in Gaza feels as though it's torn down the illusion of Western humility and exposed just how ugly humanity can be. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I grew up in Cambridge, and my early trips into London—to places like The End, Metalheadz at Leisure Lounge (I was too young for Blue Note, unfortunately), and the early days of fabric—were all formative experiences that had a huge impact on my young brain. You once used the phrase "business techno," which has since taken on a life of its own. Looking back, how do you feel about how that term has evolved, and the state of techno culture today? In all honesty, if I'm never asked about this again, it will still be too soon. As far as techno culture goes, it's like any other scene. There's tons of incredible music and people genuinely trying to contribute, and tons of shit music and posers treating it like an ATM. I don't suppose it was ever any different, or ever will be. It's just that these days the bad stuff is shoved down our throats more. You've released mixes for RA under three different aliases: Commix, Shifted and now Carrier. How do you approach each identity, and is there a thorough line between them? The lines are very blurred now. I feel like Carrier is a culmination of everything I've learned and picked up along the way. So yes, there's definitely an ongoing conversation happening. I just want to make art that's unique and personal, and that stands the test of time. There's a trajectory a lot of artists follow where they keep dining out on things they did years ago. I don’t want that. I want to constantly push forward, which means knowing when to leave things behind, however uncomfortable that might feel. What kind of spaces do you imagine Carrier's music being played in? Are you still thinking in terms of the dance floor, or has that shifted entirely? I still think of it as dance music, and I believe it's best experienced in the kind of spaces that inspired its inception—a dark room, good sound with a lot of bottom-end weight and a crowd of people who fundamentally get it. It's obviously quite (shudder) deconstructed, but at its core it's still about the club experience.
translation.blurb: Dubwise atmospherics flood Guy Brewer's third RA Mix.
